137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple

41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
20	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
21	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
22	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
23	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
24	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
25	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
26	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
27	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
28	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
29	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
30	
31	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
32	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
33	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
34	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
35	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
36	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
37	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
38	\[
39	  \begin{array}{ccc}
40	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
41	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
42	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
43	  \end{array}
44	\]
45	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
46	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
47	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
48	
49	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
50

93	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
94	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials

97	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open

99	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens

167	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
168	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
169	
170	It is convenient to factor the construction of
171	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
172	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
173	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
174	
175	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
176	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
177	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
178	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
179	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
180	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
181	\[
182	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
183	    \;=\;
184	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
185	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
186	\]
187	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
188	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
189	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
190	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
191	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
192	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
193	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
194	
195	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
196	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
197	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
198	\[
199	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
200	  \qquad
201	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
202	\]
203	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
204	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
205	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
206	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
207	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
208	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
209	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
210	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
211	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
212	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
213	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
214	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
215	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
216	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
217	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
218	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
219	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
220	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
221	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
222	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
223	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
224	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
225	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
226	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
227	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
228	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
229	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
230	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
231	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
232	
233	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
234	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
235	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
236	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
237	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
238	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
239	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
240	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
241	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
242	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
243	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
244	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
245	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
246	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
247	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
248	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
249	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
250	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
251	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
252	
253	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
254	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
255	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
256	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
257	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
258

346	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
347	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
348	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
349	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
350	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
351	  \[
352	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
353	  \]
354	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
355	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
356	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
357	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
358	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.

361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
363	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
364	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
365	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
366	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
367	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
368	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
369	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
370	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
371	\end{proof}

373	\begin{lemma}
374	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
375	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
377	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
378	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
379	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
380	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
381	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
382	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
383	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
384	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
385	  \[
386	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
387	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
388	    \;=\;
389	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
390	  \]

392	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
393	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
394	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
395	\end{lemma}

399	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
400	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
401	\end{proof}

489	  \mathlibok

491	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
492	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
493	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
494	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
495	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member

497	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
498	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation

502	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
503	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
504	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
505	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
506	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.

509	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
510	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
511	
512	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
513	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
514	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
515	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
516	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
517	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
518	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
519	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
520	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
521	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
522	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
523	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
524	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
525	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
526	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.

718	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
719	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
720	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
721	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
722	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
723	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
724	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
725	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
726	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
727	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
728	  element-level product equivalence
729	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
730	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
731	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
732	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
733	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
734	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
735	
736	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
737	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
738	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
739	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
740	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
741	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
742	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
743	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
744	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
745	  being proved.
746	\end{proof}

853	  \label{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}

858	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} vanishes,
859	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
860	  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\). The reduction uses
861	  \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) to pass to
862	  \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\), then the abelian analogue
863	  \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\) --- which states that a short complex
864	  \(S\) of abelian groups is exact iff for every \(x_2\) with \(g(x_2) = 0\) there is
865	  \(x_1\) with \(f(x_1) = x_2\) --- to pass to underlying-group exactness. The latter is
866	  the already-proved \(R\)-module exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
867	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}) transported across the product
868	  equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} with the differential match

875	  prove \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of the section complex at \(p\), i.e.\ exactness of
876	  the short complex \(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\) of abelian groups around degree
877	  \(p\). Since each term is a categorical product in \(\mathrm{Ab}\), exactness is
878	  governed by \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\): it is equivalent to
879	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) on the
880	  underlying group elements. Transport this surjectivity-onto-kernel statement across

886	  \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.

1177	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1178	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single

1180	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1181	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1182	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1183	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1184	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1185	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1186	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1187	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of

1189	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1190	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1191	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via

1195	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1196	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived

\begin{lemma}
[Coface match: the section {\v C}ech differential is the localisation differential]
  \label{lem:section_cech_coface_match}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCofaceMatch}
  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_module_complex}
  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the degree-\(p\)
  differential of the section {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- the degree-\(p\) map of
  the alternating-coface-map complex
  \(\operatorname{AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex}\) of the cosimplicial
  object \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\) --- unfolds to the alternating sum
  \[
    d^p \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\, \delta_j,
    \qquad
    \delta_j = \operatorname{Pi.lift}\bigl(\sigma \mapsto
      \pi_{\sigma \circ d_j} \;\circ\;
      \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{res}_j)\bigr),
  \]
  whose \(j\)-th coface \(\delta_j\) projects to the coordinate \(\sigma \circ d_j\) and
  applies the presheaf restriction
  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\). Under the
  product equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} and the
  multi-index intersection identity \(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), each such
  restriction is the away-localisation comparison
  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\)), i.e.\ the coface
  \(\operatorname{dCoface}\) of \(D^\bullet\). Hence \(d^p\) is exactly the localisation
  differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}, transported across the product
  equivalence.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
  Unfold the degree-\(p\) map of
  \(\operatorname{alternatingCofaceMapComplex}\) on
  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\): by construction it is the alternating sum
  \(\sum_j (-1)^j\) of the cosimplicial cofaces, and the cosimplicial map at coface
  \(d_j\) is the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\) whose \(\sigma\)-component projects to
  \(\sigma \circ d_j\) and restricts along
  \(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)}) \leq \bigcap_l D(s_{(\sigma \circ d_j)(l)})\). The
  intersection identity \(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\) rewrites both products of
  basic opens as single basic opens \(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j}) \supseteq D(s_\sigma)\),
  and \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\) identifies the presheaf
  restriction between the corresponding section groups with the away comparison
  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\); this is precisely
  \(\operatorname{dCoface}\). Summing with signs and transporting along the product
  equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} matches \(d^p\) with
  \(\operatorname{dDiff}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Abstract cosimplicial unfold of the section {\v C}ech differential]
  \label{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_objD_apply,
    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechFaceRestr}
  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open cover. For a multi-index
  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(q+2) \to I\) and a face index
  \(i \in \mathrm{Fin}(q+2)\), write
  \[
    \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i) :
      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\Bigr)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)}\Bigr)
  \]
  for the \(i\)-th presheaf face restriction --- the value of \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
  inclusion of opens
  \(\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)} \subseteq \bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\)
  obtained from the order-preserving coface \(d_i\) of the simplex category. Then,
  read through the product equivalence
  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the underlying group action of the
  degree-\(q\) differential
  \(\operatorname{objD} q\) of the alternating-coface-map complex
  \(\operatorname{AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex}
  (\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\,\mathcal{U}\,\mathcal{F})\) is the alternating
  sum of the face restrictions applied to the deleted-face coordinates:
  \[
    \bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q+1}\,(\operatorname{objD} q\, t)\bigr)_\sigma
      \;=\;
      \sum_{i = 0}^{q+1} (-1)^i \,
        \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
          \Bigl(\bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q}\, t\bigr)_{\sigma \circ d_i}\Bigr).
  \]
  This is \emph{purely cosimplicial}: it records the shape of the section {\v C}ech
  differential as an alternating sum of presheaf restrictions, with \emph{no}
  sheaf-theoretic or localisation identification of those restrictions. It is the
  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} matches once the tilde-bridge
  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match} is supplied.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
  Unfold the degree-\(q\) map of \(\operatorname{alternatingCofaceMapComplex}\) on
  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\): by construction \(\operatorname{objD} q\)
  is the alternating sum \(\sum_{i} (-1)^i\, d^i\) of the cosimplicial cofaces, each
  coface \(d^i\) being the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\) whose \(\sigma\)-component
  projects to the coordinate \(\sigma \circ d_i\) and post-composes with the presheaf
  restriction \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\). Reading the
  \(\sigma\)-coordinate through the product equivalence (the coordinate-projection
  restatement \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\)) commutes the projection
  past the categorical sum: applying the sum of \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-morphisms to an element
  distributes over the sum (the finite-sum-application identity
  \(\operatorname{ab\_hom\_finsetSum\_apply}\)), and each summand becomes
  \((-1)^i\) times the face restriction of the \((\sigma \circ d_i)\)-coordinate. The
  per-coordinate projection of the cosimplicial coface is the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\)
  computation \(\operatorname{Pi.lift\_π}\), which identifies the projected coface with
  \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) precomposed with the projection to
  \(\sigma \circ d_i\). Assembling the signs yields the stated alternating sum.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Coface match: the section {\v C}ech differential is the localisation differential]
  \label{lem:section_cech_coface_match}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCofaceMatch}
  \uses{lem:section_cech_objd_apply, def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_module_complex}
  Take \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\), the standard affine cover \(\mathcal{U}\)
  associated with a spanning family \(s : \iota \to R\), and \(\mathcal{F} =
  \widetilde{M}\) the quasi-coherent sheaf associated with an \(R\)-module \(M\) (the
  active target; see the note below on a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\)). In
  the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the degree-\(p\)
  differential of the section {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), read through the product
  equivalence, is exactly the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of the
  module complex \(D^\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}.
  Concretely, under the per-coordinate identifications each section group
  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\) and each face
  restriction \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) is the away-localisation
  coface \(\operatorname{dCoface}\), so the alternating sum of face restrictions of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} becomes the alternating sum of localisation
  cofaces, i.e.\ \(\operatorname{dDiff}\).
\end{lemma}

% NOTE: the active target is the tilde case F = tilde M, where the per-coordinate
% AddEquiv to LocalizedModule and the naturality square come for free from
% qcohRestriction_eq_comparison. The general quasi-coherent F is reduced to this case
% via F ≅ tilde(Γ F) (Stacks Tag 01I8), the affine equivalence
% QCoh(Spec R) ≃ Mod(R), which is the separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded
% in def:qcoh_sections_localized.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:section_cech_objd_apply, def:section_cech_module_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
  The proof layers in two steps.

  \emph{(i) Abstract unfold.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}, the
  \(\sigma\)-coordinate of the degree-\(p\) differential, read through the product
  equivalence, is the alternating sum
  \(\sum_{i} (-1)^i\, \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) of presheaf face
  restrictions applied to the deleted-face coordinates. This fixes the shape of the
  differential without any sheaf identification of the restrictions.

  \emph{(ii) Tilde \(\mathcal{F}\)-bridge.} For \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) each
  face restriction is identified with the localisation coface
  \(\operatorname{SectionCechModule.dCoface}\) by transporting along a per-coordinate
  isomorphism. The section group
  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and the localised module
  \(M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}
  s_\sigma)\, M\) both localise \(M\) at the submonoid of powers of
  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\): the former by
  \(\operatorname{qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}\) together with the multi-index
  intersection identity \(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\) (which rewrites
  \(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\) as the single basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\)), the latter
  by construction. Hence there is a canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism
  \[
    \varphi_\sigma :
      \operatorname{ToType}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\bigr)
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
      M_{s_\sigma},
  \]
  the comparison of the two \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) structures, unique by
  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) and supplied by
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\). The naturality square
  \[
    \varphi_\sigma \;\circ\; \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
      \;=\;
      \operatorname{dCoface} \;\circ\; \varphi_{\sigma \circ d_i}
  \]
  is exactly \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the presheaf restriction of
  \(\widetilde{M}\) between the two section groups is the away-localisation comparison
  of the two localisation units. Substituting (ii) into (i) and summing with signs
  identifies the section {\v C}ech differential with \(\operatorname{dDiff}\).

  The infrastructure this step owns is the accessor reconciliation: the section
  {\v C}ech complex reads sections through the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued accessor
  \(\bigl((\operatorname{toPresheafOfModules}(\operatorname{Spec} R)).\mathrm{obj}\,
  \mathcal{F}\bigr).\mathrm{presheaf}.\mathrm{obj}(\operatorname{op}(D(s_\sigma)))\),
  whereas the proved localisation facts of
  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} are stated for the
  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\)-valued sections of
  \(\operatorname{modulesSpecToSheaf}\) / \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen}\). Reconciling
  these two views of ``the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(D(s_\sigma)\)'' into the
  single \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) \(\varphi_\sigma\) and its naturality square is the
  content of this bridge.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Abelian-group exactness criterion for the section {\v C}ech complex]
  \label{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechAbExact}
  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
  For \(1 \leq p\) the homology of the section {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} vanishes,
  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\). The reduction uses
  \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) to pass to
  \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\), then the abelian analogue
  \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\) --- which states that a short complex
  \(S\) of abelian groups is exact iff for every \(x_2\) with \(g(x_2) = 0\) there is
  \(x_1\) with \(f(x_1) = x_2\) --- to pass to underlying-group exactness. The latter is
  the already-proved \(R\)-module exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}) transported across the product
  equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} with the differential match
  of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
  Fix \(p \geq 1\). By \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) it suffices to
  prove \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of the section complex at \(p\), i.e.\ exactness of
  the short complex \(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\) of abelian groups around degree
  \(p\). Since each term is a categorical product in \(\mathrm{Ab}\), exactness is
  governed by \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\): it is equivalent to
  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) on the
  underlying group elements. Transport this surjectivity-onto-kernel statement across
  the element-level product equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
  (which identifies the product groups with \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\)) and the
  coface match of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match} (which identifies the
  differentials with \(\operatorname{dDiff}\)); the resulting statement is exactly
  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(\operatorname{dDiff})\) in positive degree, which is
  \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Abelian-group exactness criterion for the section {\v C}ech complex]
  \label{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechAbExact,
    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_isZero_homology_of_objD_exact,
    AlgebraicGeometry.ab_hom_finsetSum_apply}
  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_coface_match,
    lem:section_cech_module_exact}
  For \(1 \leq p\) the homology of the section {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} vanishes,
  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\). The argument splits into a homological
  precursor and an unconditional ladder transport.
  \begin{itemize}
    \item The precursor \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_isZero\_homology\_of\_objD\_exact}\)
      is pure homological algebra: \emph{given}
      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of two
      consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
      \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\), the homology of the section complex at degree
      \(q+1\) is zero. It chains
      \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\),
      \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.exactAt\_iff'}\) (to expose the short complex
      around the node), and the abelian-group exactness criterion
      \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff\_function\_exact}\) (which states that
      a short complex of abelian groups is exact iff for every \(x_2\) with
      \(g(x_2) = 0\) there is \(x_1\) with \(f(x_1) = x_2\)).
    \item The unconditional \(\operatorname{sectionCechAbExact}\) discharges that
      hypothesis by transporting the already-proved \(R\)-module exactness
      \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
      Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the degreewise additive
      isomorphism, using the ladder-transport lemma
      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact.of\_ladder\_addEquiv\_of\_exact}\).
  \end{itemize}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:section_cech_objd_apply, lem:section_cech_product_equiv,
    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
  Fix \(p = q + 1 \geq 1\). By the precursor
  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_isZero\_homology\_of\_objD\_exact}\) it suffices to
  exhibit \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of the
  two consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
  \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\) of the section complex.

  This exactness is obtained by the ladder transport
  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact.of\_ladder\_addEquiv\_of\_exact}\). The vertical maps
  of the ladder are the degreewise additive isomorphisms
  \(e_p = \prod_\sigma \varphi_\sigma \circ \operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_p\):
  the product equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} followed by the
  coordinatewise comparison \(\varphi_\sigma\) identifying each section group with the
  localised module \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The two commuting squares of the ladder are the
  coface match: the abstract unfold of the differential as an alternating sum of face
  restrictions (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}, the decl
  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_objD\_apply}\)) together with the per-coordinate
  identification of each face restriction with the localisation coface
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}) shows
  \(e_{p+1} \circ \operatorname{objD} p = \operatorname{dDiff}^{\,p} \circ e_p\). The
  horizontal exactness fed into the transport is the \(R\)-module exactness
  \(H = \operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
  Transporting \(H\) across the additive ladder yields the desired
  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the \(\operatorname{objD}\) maps, discharging the
  hypothesis of the precursor.
\end{proof}
